text: Phenolic compounds are among the largest classes of emissions from biomass burning and have been observed to form secondary organic aerosol (aqSOAerosol formed through interactions between gaseous compounds and aqueous acidic droplets) in the aqueous atmosphere through relatively rapid reactions. Despite their known importance, the detailed mechanisms and resulting products of these reactions have not yet been fully explored in the literature. In this study, we investigate the aqueous-phase oxidation of the primary phenolic compounds phenol, guaiacol, and syringol by two of the primary aqueous-phase oxidants: the triplet excited state of an aromatic carbonyl (3C*) and the hydroxyl radical (· OH). Aerosol mass spectrometry (AMS), nanospray desorption electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (nano-DESI MS), and ion chromatography (IC) are used to quantify and characterize the production of hundreds of low-volatility species derived from these reactions including oligomers consisting of up to 6 rings, monomer and oligomeric species functionalized with carbonyl, carboxyl, or hydroxyl groups, as well as small organic acid anions including formate, acetate, oxalate, and malate. The average atomic oxygen-to-carbon (O / C) ratios for phenolic aqSOA fall in the range of 0.85–1.23 similar to that of low-volatility oxygenated organic aerosol (LV-OOA) observed in ambient air. Regardless of oxidant, the aqSOA products derived from the oxidation of each precursor are qualitatively similar, but 3C*-mediated reactions are faster than · OH-mediated reactions and produce a higher abundance of oligomers and hydroxylated species at any given extent of reaction. The thermodenuder profile of the aqSOA is affected by oligomer content as well as O / C ratio, and the aqSOA has increased light absorption in the UV–visible that is analogous to secondary brown carbon. Overall, these results indicate that the aqueous-phase oxidation of phenolic compounds contributes to secondary brown carbon formation, particularly in biomass-burning-influenced environments.
